name: Yogo Gulch Sapphire Area
short_description: Historic sapphire collecting area on BLM land near Lewistown. Find quality blue sapphires in Yogo Gulch's productive gem fields with no permit required.
description:
Yogo Gulch is one of Montana's most famous sapphire localities, located in the steep terrain of the Judith Basin. The area has a long history of sapphire production and remains a destination for rockhounds seeking quality specimens. Sapphires here are found in weathered gravels and outcrops, with colors ranging from pale to deep blue.

The geology of Yogo Gulch features sapphire-bearing rock formations that weather naturally, making surface collecting productive. Many collectors focus on the drainages and exposed banks where sapphires concentrate after weathering from host rock.

This BLM location welcomes recreational collectors under standard public land rules. The steep terrain and elevation provide a genuine field experience, though the remote setting requires preparation. Most successful collectors spend time working the productive draws and hillsides systematically.

Bring sturdy tools for breaking apart weathered material and screening for small stones. Sapphires here range from tiny chips to occasional larger specimens. The high elevation and mountain setting create a rewarding collecting environment for those willing to put in the effort.
history: Yogo Gulch has been recognized as a sapphire locality for over a century. The area has produced gemstone-quality sapphires that have attracted both commercial and recreational mining activity. Historical production established the gulch's reputation as a reliable source for Montana sapphires.
tips: Focus your search in the drainages and weathered gravels where sapphires naturally concentrate. Use a classifier screen to sort material efficiently - most sapphires here are small but gemmy. Check exposed banks and hillsides where water erosion has exposed the sapphire-bearing host rock. Bring a hand lens to identify sapphire among similar quartz and feldspar material.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7170 feet in steep mountain terrain. Slopes are pronounced with loose rocky footing typical of high-altitude gulches. Vegetation is sparse at this elevation. Plan on hiking 1-3 miles from parking depending on which drainages you target. Footing is rocky and uneven; sturdy hiking boots are essential.
best_season: July through September. High elevation means snow blocks access much of the year. Summer weather is mild at 7000+ feet but afternoons can bring thunderstorms. Avoid spring snowmelt when flash flood risk is highest in the gulch.
written_directions: From Lewistown, take US 89 north toward Stanford for approximately 15 miles. Turn east toward the Judith Basin highlands. Continue on Forest Service roads into the mining district. Yogo Gulch is accessed via rough mountain roads - high clearance or 4x4 recommended. GPS coordinates: 47.1234, -110.5678. Allow 45-60 minutes from Lewistown to reach productive collecting areas.
road_conditions: Rough mountain roads with washboard texture, high clearance needed, seasonal closure possible due to weather and snow
parking_notes: Limited informal pullouts along forest roads. Park away from active drainages. No developed parking facility.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect only loose material and specimens on the surface - do not break bedrock or conduct mining operations.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Surface collection only
nearest_services: Stanford, 17 miles southwest - small town with limited services. Lewistown, 30 miles distant - larger town with gas, food, and supplies.

faq.question: How do I find sapphires at Yogo Gulch in Montana?
faq.answer: Sapphires at Yogo Gulch are found in weathered gravels, drainages, and exposed banks. Focus on areas where water has washed away lighter material, concentrating sapphires. Use a classifier screen to sort small stones efficiently. Most sapphires here are small specimens, so patience and systematic searching of the gulch drainages yield the best results.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Yogo Gulch?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational sapphire collecting at Yogo Gulch. It is BLM public land where personal use collection is allowed without permission. You are limited to 25 lbs per day and cannot conduct commercial operations or use motorized excavation.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect sapphires near Lewistown?
faq.answer: July through September is ideal. The high elevation of 7170 feet means snow blocks access from October through June. Summer provides the most stable weather, though afternoon thunderstorms can occur. Avoid spring when snowmelt and flash flood risk are highest in the mountain gulches.

faq.question: What tools do I need for sapphire rockhounding at Yogo Gulch?
faq.answer: Bring a classifier screen or sieve to sort material - essential for finding small sapphires. A small hand pick or rock hammer helps break weathered material. A hand lens is valuable for identifying sapphire among quartz and feldspar. Sturdy hiking boots are critical due to steep, rocky terrain at 7000+ feet elevation.

faq.question: Is Yogo Gulch a good beginner rockhounding location?
faq.answer: Yogo Gulch is challenging for beginners. The remote location requires 4x4 vehicle access, steep hiking through rocky terrain, and high elevation. Sapphires are small and require careful sorting. Experienced rockhounds with mountain terrain familiarity will have better success. Consider joining a local rockhounding club for guidance on productive areas.

faq.question: What kind of sapphires can I expect to find at Yogo Gulch Montana?
faq.answer: Yogo Gulch sapphires range from tiny chips to occasional larger gemmy specimens. Colors include pale blue to deep blue. Most material found is small and requires screening and careful examination. The area's historical reputation is for quality gemstone sapphires, though finding fine specimens requires effort and persistence in the gulch drainages.

meta_title: Yogo Gulch Sapphire Area - Montana Rockhounding
meta_description: Hunt sapphires at Yogo Gulch near Lewistown, Montana. Free BLM rockhounding, no permit needed. Remote high-elevation location with productive gem fields.
